d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: U.S. President Thomas Jefferson refused to pay tribute to North African Barbary pirates, leading Tripoli's Pasha Yusuf Karamanli to declare war. Jefferson dispatched the newly-built U.S. Navy fleet to blockade Tripoli harbor and, in 1805, coordinated with ground forces to capture Derna. This four-year war established the U.S. Navy's presence in the Mediterranean and ended the long-standing policy of paying tribute to pirates.

v.t: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland formed
v.s: Under the Act of Union 1800, the Kingdom of Ireland formally merged with the Kingdom of Great Britain to create the 'United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.' The Irish Parliament was dissolved, and Ireland received 100 seats in the London Parliament. Saint Patrick's Cross was added to the Union Jack, marking strengthened British control over Ireland while also sowing seeds for the Irish nationalist movement.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: France and Britain signed a peace treaty at Amiens in northern France, temporarily ending the French Revolutionary Wars and the first phase of the Napoleonic Wars. Britain returned most colonies seized from France and its allies, while France recognized Maltese independence and agreed to withdraw from Naples and Rome. Though lasting only a year, the treaty brought brief peace to Europe and allowed France to consolidate internal rule.

v.t: France establishes the Legion of Honour
v.s: Napoleon Bonaparte created the Legion of Honour to recognize outstanding achievements in military and civil service. It was France's first non-monarchical order of merit, based on merit rather than birth, aimed at uniting the emerging elite. It remains France's highest honor.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: The United States purchased approximately 2.14 million square kilometers of Louisiana territory from France for $15 million (about 3 cents per acre) through a treaty signed in Paris. This vast land stretched from the Mississippi River to the Rocky Mountains, doubling U.S. territory. President Jefferson promptly dispatched the Lewis and Clark Expedition to explore the new territory, securing Mississippi River navigation rights and laying the foundation for American westward expansion.

Due to disagreements in implementing the Treaty of Amiens and Napoleon's ambitions over Malta, Switzerland, and other regions, Britain declared war on France on May 18, marking the second phase of the Napoleonic Wars. Britain formed the Third Coalition with Russia and Austria to contain French expansion. The ensuing conflict lasted over a decade and fundamentally reshaped Europe's political landscape.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor at Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris, proclaiming the First French Empire. He took the crown from Pope Pius VII, asserting that his power came from the people, not the Church. Napoleon subsequently promulgated the Napoleonic Code, consolidating revolutionary achievements, ushering France into the imperial era, and intensifying the Napoleonic Wars.

Jean-Jacques Dessalines declared Haiti's formal independence, establishing the world's first republic founded by former black slaves. The Haitian Revolution, which began in 1791, saw over a decade of fierce fighting that ultimately defeated French, Spanish, and British forces. Haiti's independence dealt a heavy blow to colonialism and slavery, profoundly influencing abolitionist movements in the Caribbean and the Americas.

The Napoleonic Code (Civil Code) came into effect, establishing principles of equality before the law, inviolability of private property, and freedom of contract. It blended revolutionary ideals with Roman legal tradition. This code not only formed the foundation of France's modern legal system but also served as a model for civil law systems across Europe and the world.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: The British Royal Navy under Admiral Horatio Nelson engaged the combined Franco-Spanish fleet of 33 ships with 27 ships off Cape Trafalgar, Spain. Using his 'Nelson Touch' tactic of breaking the enemy line, Nelson routed the enemy fleet and captured multiple ships, but was fatally shot. This victory established British global naval supremacy for over a century, forcing Napoleon to abandon his invasion plans and adopt the Continental System instead.

v.t: Battle of Austerlitz (Battle of the Three Emperors)
v.s: Napoleon decisively defeated the combined Russo-Austrian army led by Tsar Alexander I and Holy Roman Emperor Francis II at Austerlitz (today's Slavkov u Brna, Czech Republic). The French outmaneuvered a larger force, inflicting over 27,000 casualties. This victory forced Austria to sign the Treaty of Pressburg and effectively dissolved the Holy Roman Empire. Napoleon subsequently established the Confederation of the Rhine, bringing 16 German states under French protection.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.t: Dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire
v.s: After the Battle of Austerlitz, Napoleon forced Austrian Emperor Francis II to relinquish the Holy Roman imperial title, retaining only the title of Emperor of Austria. This nearly thousand-year-old empire formally ended. Napoleon then established the Confederation of the Rhine and reorganized German territories, shattering the old order and laying groundwork for eventual German unification.

v.t: Napoleon issues the Berlin Decree (Continental System)
v.s: To cripple Britain's economy, Napoleon issued the Berlin Decree, prohibiting European continental nations from trading, corresponding, or maintaining contact with Britain. All British goods and vessels were subject to seizure. The Continental System dealt a blow to the British economy but also provoked resistance from Portugal, Russia, and others, ultimately contributing to the collapse of Napoleon's empire.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.t: Fulton's steamboat makes first commercial voyage
v.s: American inventor Robert Fulton's steamboat 'Clermont' completed a round trip on the Hudson River from New York to Albany, covering about 480 km in 32 hours. This was the first commercially successful steam-powered vessel. The steamboat revolutionized water transportation, boosting global trade, population movement, and inland development.

v.t: British Slave Trade Act abolishes slave trade
v.s: The British Parliament passed the Abolition of the Slave Trade Act, prohibiting British ships and colonies from engaging in the slave trade effective January 1, 1808. The act was the result of a long abolitionist movement led by figures like William Wilberforce. Subsequently, the Royal Navy intercepted slave ships off West Africa and encouraged other European nations to follow suit.

Napoleon met Tsar Alexander I on a raft in the middle of the Neman River to sign the Treaties of Tilsit. Russia left the anti-French coalition, recognized French territorial changes in Europe, and joined the Continental System. Prussia lost half its territory and became a French satellite. The Franco-Russian alliance temporarily consolidated Napoleon's European hegemony but sowed the seeds for his disastrous 1812 invasion of Russia.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.t: French invasion of Spain (Peninsular War begins)
v.s: Using the pretext of securing French troop passage to invade Portugal, Napoleon sent forces into Madrid, forced King Ferdinand VII to abdicate, and installed his brother Joseph Bonaparte as King of Spain. This sparked a massive popular uprising, and Britain dispatched Arthur Wellesley (later Duke of Wellington) to assist. The Peninsular War lasted six years, bogging France down in guerrilla warfare and marking a turning point in Napoleon's decline.

Ludwig van Beethoven presented a four-hour marathon concert at the Theater an der Wien, premiering his Symphony No. 5 ('Fate') and Symphony No. 6 ('Pastoral'). The opening 'fate motif' (da-da-da-dum) became one of the most famous melodies in Western music. Beethoven's hearing was severely deteriorated, yet he continued composing and conducting. The work embodies the indomitable human spirit in struggle against fate.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: Napoleon's French army of about 150,000 men clashed with Archduke Charles' Austrian main force of about 140,000 at Wagram on the Danube. After two days of fierce fighting, the French prevailed, with Austrian losses of about 40,000. Austria was forced to sign the Treaty of Schönbrunn, ceding territory and joining the Continental System. This victory consolidated Napoleon's control over Central Europe, but at a heavy cost.

English naturalist Charles Darwin was born in Shrewsbury, England. He later embarked on a five-year global voyage aboard HMS Beagle, collecting extensive biological and geological specimens. In 1859, he published 'On the Origin of Species,' proposing natural selection, which revolutionized humanity's understanding of life's evolution.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: Father Miguel Hidalgo rang the church bell in Dolores, Mexico, issuing the famous 'Grito de Dolores,' calling on indigenous people and mestizos to rise up against Spanish colonial rule. The insurgent army quickly gathered tens of thousands and marched toward Mexico City. Although Hidalgo was captured and executed the following year, this war launched Mexico's independence movement, culminating in independence in 1821.

Prussian King Friedrich Wilhelm III, at the urging of Wilhelm von Humboldt, founded the Friedrich-Wilhelms-Universität (today's Humboldt University of Berlin). Humboldt's philosophy of combining teaching with research, emphasizing academic freedom and basic research, was swiftly adopted by universities across Europe and America, becoming the model for modern research universities.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: Textile workers in Nottinghamshire, England, angered by machines replacing labor leading to unemployment and wage cuts, organized to destroy textile machinery, led by a figure called 'King Ludd.' The movement spread to Yorkshire, Lancashire, and other industrial areas. The British government deployed troops to suppress it and enacted laws making machine-breaking a capital offense. The Luddite movement exemplified early worker resistance to the negative consequences of the Industrial Revolution.

Paraguayans rose up, overthrowing Spanish colonial rule and establishing an independent governing council. Unlike much of Latin America, Paraguay's independence was relatively peaceful, avoiding prolonged civil war. After independence, José Gaspar Rodríguez de Francia became supreme dictator, implementing an isolationist policy that maintained stability for decades.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: Napoleon led approximately 600,000 troops (including French and allied forces) across the Neman River to invade Russia. The Russians employed scorched-earth tactics, retreating while drawing the enemy deeper. In September, French forces entered Moscow but found it burning and without supplies. With winter approaching, Napoleon was forced to retreat, suffering attacks from Russian forces and severe cold; only about 30,000 survived. This catastrophic campaign crippled Napoleon's empire and led to the formation of the Sixth Coalition.

The United States declared war on Britain due to impressment of American sailors, trade restrictions affecting U.S. shipping, and British support for Native American resistance to westward expansion. Fighting occurred mainly along the Canadian border, the Atlantic, and the U.S. South. Although the war ended in a stalemate (Treaty of Ghent restoring pre-war borders), the U.S. gained international respect and nationalist sentiment surged, while British forces burned Washington D.C. including the White House.

v.t: First volume of Grimm's Fairy Tales published
v.s: German linguists Jacob and Wilhelm Grimm published the first volume of 'Children's and Household Tales,' collecting 86 stories including 'The Frog Prince' and 'Hansel and Gretel.' They gathered tales through oral collection and literary editing, aiming to preserve German cultural heritage. The collection became a world literary classic, translated into hundreds of languages.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: The Sixth Coalition's combined forces of Russia, Prussia, Austria, and Sweden, about 300,000 strong, engaged Napoleon's approximately 190,000 French troops near Leipzig in Saxony. This was one of the most intense battles of the Napoleonic Wars, lasting three days. Outnumbered and betrayed by Saxon troops who switched sides mid-battle, the French lost over 70,000 men. Napoleon was forced to retreat across the Rhine, the Confederation of the Rhine collapsed, and French territory lay exposed to the Coalition.

v.t: Jane Austen publishes Pride and Prejudice
v.s: English novelist Jane Austen's classic 'Pride and Prejudice' was published. Through the marriage stories of the five Bennet sisters, the novel vividly portrays the lives and romantic ideals of the early 19th-century English gentry, satirizing marriage for money and class prejudice. Extensively adapted into films and television series, it remains one of the most beloved works in English literature.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: The Sixth Coalition entered Paris, and Napoleon signed his abdication at the Palace of Fontainebleau, ending the First French Empire. Under the Treaty of Fontainebleau, Napoleon retained the imperial title but his domain was limited to the island of Elba in the Mediterranean, with an annual income of 2 million francs. The Bourbon dynasty was restored under Louis XVIII. Napoleon's abdication allowed European powers to convene the Congress of Vienna to redraw borders.

v.t: Stephenson builds first practical steam locomotive
v.s: British engineer George Stephenson completed the first practical steam locomotive, 'Blücher,' at Killingworth Colliery. It pulled eight wagons carrying 30 tons of coal at 6.5 km/h on an inclined railway. The invention of the steam locomotive laid the foundation for railway transport, ushering in the railway age in transportation history.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: Napoleon escaped from Elba and returned to France to rebuild the Hundred Days Empire. The British and Prussian armies, under the Duke of Wellington and Field Marshal Blücher, defeated Napoleon at Waterloo, Belgium. Lacking timely reinforcements and hampered by tactical errors, the French lost about 30,000 men. Napoleon abdicated again and was exiled to St. Helena in the Atlantic, ending the First French Empire for good.

v.t: Congress of Vienna establishes the Concert of Europe
v.s: The anti-French coalition powers convened in Vienna, Austria, led by Austrian Chancellor Metternich, to redraw Europe's map after the Napoleonic Wars. The conference established the principle of 'legitimism,' restoring France to its 1792 borders, and created a five-power (Britain, Russia, Austria, Prussia, France) consultation mechanism. This system maintained overall peace in Europe for nearly 40 years until the Crimean War in 1853.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: The 1815 eruption of Mount Tambora in Indonesia (the largest volcanic eruption in recorded history) released ash into the stratosphere, blocking sunlight and causing global temperatures to plummet. In 1816, the U.S. Northeast and Canada experienced June snowfall and frozen rivers, while Europe and Asia also suffered severe cold, frost, and crop failures, triggering widespread famine. This was the most severe volcanic winter event in modern history.

The Congress of Tucumán formally declared independence from Spain for the United Provinces of the Río de la Plata (modern Argentina). The May Revolution of 1810 had already established an autonomous government; after six years of struggle, independence was achieved. Post-independence Argentina descended into a long civil war between centralists and federalists until the federal republic system was established in 1853.

German Baron Karl von Drais invented the 'Laufmaschine' (running machine), also called the draisine, the earliest prototype of the bicycle. It was a wooden two-wheeled device propelled by the rider's feet pushing against the ground. In 1839, Scottish blacksmith Kirkpatrick Macmillan added pedals and crank mechanisms. John Starley's 1885 'safety bicycle' established the modern bicycle design.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.t: Battle of Chacabuco - Key Chilean independence battle
v.s: José de San Martín led the Army of the Andes (composed of Argentinian and Chilean exiles) to defeat Spanish royalist forces at Chacabuco, laying the foundation for Chilean independence. Chile formally declared independence the following year. San Martín then proceeded north to liberate Peru, becoming a central figure in South America's southern independence movement.

The United States and Britain signed the Rush-Bagot Treaty (also known as the Great Lakes Demilitarization Agreement), drastically reducing naval forces on the Great Lakes and Lake Champlain, with each side retaining only one armed vessel for law enforcement. This was the first true disarmament agreement between the U.S. and Britain, easing border tensions and initiating two centuries of peaceful coexistence.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: French physician René Laennec, uncomfortable pressing his ear directly to female patients' chests, rolled a paper tube and placed it on a patient's chest, finding the heartbeat clearly audible. This led to his invention of the wooden monaural stethoscope. This invention greatly advanced cardiopulmonary diagnosis, enabling doctors to distinguish breath and heart sounds and detect abnormalities. Laennec subsequently published 'De l'Auscultation Médiate,' founding modern auscultation.

The United States and Britain signed a convention establishing the 49th parallel as the border between the U.S. and British North America (Canada) from Lake of the Woods to the Rocky Mountains. It also provided for joint occupation of the Oregon Country for 10 years. This was another achievement in peaceful resolution of border disputes, laying the groundwork for the eventual delineation of the North American continent.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: British East India Company official Sir Stamford Raffles signed a treaty with the Sultan of Johor to establish a trading post at the southern tip of the Malay Peninsula. Singapore, strategically located at the choke point of the Malacca Strait with a natural deep-water harbor, rapidly developed into a free port and entrepôt center. From a small fishing village, it grew into Britain's most important Far Eastern colony and remains one of the world's busiest ports today.

v.t: Panic of 1819 - first major U.S. financial crisis
v.s: America's first severe economic crisis since independence erupted due to the Second Bank tightening credit, declining European demand for U.S. agricultural products, and the collapse of western land speculation. Numerous banks failed, factories shut down, and unemployment soared. The Panic lasted until 1821 and prompted banking reforms and stricter financial regulation.

d.u: Emperor Renzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yongyan)

v.s: The U.S. Congress passed legislation admitting Missouri as a slave state while simultaneously carving Maine out of Massachusetts as a free state to maintain Senate balance. It also prohibited slavery in the Louisiana Purchase territory north of 36°30' (except Missouri). This was America's first major compromise over slavery expansion, but it failed to resolve underlying tensions, instead sowing seeds for future conflict.

Danish physicist Hans Christian Ørsted discovered during a lecture that an electric current flowing through a wire could deflect a nearby compass needle, first demonstrating that electricity could produce magnetism. This experimental revelation of the connection between electricity and magnetism laid the foundation for electromagnetism, directly inspiring subsequent work by Ampère, Faraday, and others.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: Agustín de Iturbide, a former Spanish officer who switched to supporting independence, led the 'Army of the Three Guarantees' into Mexico City and signed the Treaty of Córdoba, formally declaring Mexican independence. Iturbide then crowned himself Emperor (Agustín I), establishing the First Mexican Empire, which lasted less than two years before Mexico became a republic.

The secret Greek organization 'Filiki Eteria' launched an uprising in the Peloponnese that rapidly spread across Greece. The Greeks fought against 400 years of Ottoman rule, garnering sympathy and support from European powers, especially Russia, Britain, and France. The war continued until 1829, when Greece gained autonomy through the Treaty of Adrianople and formal independence in 1830.

British scientist Michael Faraday built the world's first electric motor at the Royal Institution. By placing a magnet near a current-carrying wire, he achieved continuous rotation, first converting electrical energy to mechanical energy. This invention provided the fundamental principles for later electrical applications such as trams, elevators, and machine tools.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: Portuguese Crown Prince Pedro (later Pedro I) declared Brazil's independence from Portugal with the famous cry 'Independence or Death!' in São Paulo, establishing the Empire of Brazil. The process was relatively peaceful since the Portuguese royal court had relocated to Brazil (1808-1821). Brazil was the only Latin American country to maintain a monarchy until the republican revolution of 1889.

v.t: Ecuador and Venezuela join Gran Colombia
v.s: After Simón Bolívar's forces liberated Quito, Ecuador and Venezuela joined the Republic of Gran Colombia (a federation of Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, and Panama). Bolívar was elected president. Gran Colombia was the boldest political experiment of Latin America's independence movement but disintegrated in 1830 due to internal divisions.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: U.S. President James Monroe in his Seventh Annual Message to Congress articulated 'America for the Americans,' declaring the U.S. would not interfere in European affairs nor tolerate European colonization or intervention in the newly independent American nations. Initially reliant on the British Royal Navy for enforcement, the Monroe Doctrine became a cornerstone of U.S. foreign policy, later invoked to justify interventions in Latin America.

v.t: Federal Republic of Central America formed
v.s: Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica united to form the United Provinces of Central America (later the Federal Republic of Central America), separating from Spain and Mexico. The federation adopted a republican system with its capital in Guatemala City. Due to severe regional economic and political conflicts, the federation dissolved between 1838-1840, splitting into five independent states.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: The British East India Company, citing Burmese expansion into Assam threatening British India's borders, launched the First Anglo-Burmese War. British forces captured Rangoon and forced Burma to sign the Treaty of Yandabo, ceding Assam, Manipur, and coastal Tenasserim, paying £1 million in indemnity, and accepting a British resident. This war marked the beginning of British penetration and colonization of Burma.

Antonio José de Sucre's South American independence army defeated Spanish royalist forces on the plains of Ayacucho, Peru, capturing Spanish Viceroy La Serna. This battle marked the definitive end of Spanish colonial rule in mainland South America, regarded by Latin American historians as 'the endpoint of the American wars of independence.' Peru and Bolivia subsequently gained independence.

English bricklayer Joseph Aspdin of Yorkshire patented 'Portland cement.' He heated a mixture of limestone and clay at high temperatures, then ground the resulting clinker into a fine powder that hardened when mixed with water. Named for its color resemblance to Portland stone, Portland cement is the core component of modern concrete, making reinforced concrete buildings and dams possible—one of the most significant material innovations in construction history.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.t: World's first public railway - Stockton & Darlington Railway opens
v.s: George Stephenson's designed world's first public railway opened between Stockton and Darlington, England. The train, pulled by Stephenson's steam locomotive 'Locomotion No. 1,' reached a top speed of about 24 km/h, carrying 450 passengers and freight. The railway revolutionized transportation, driving the Industrial Revolution and urbanization.

Following the sudden death of Tsar Alexander I, a group of noble army officers (the Decembrists) exploited the succession crisis to stage an uprising on Senate Square in St. Petersburg, demanding abolition of serfdom and a constitutional monarchy. Nicholas I suppressed the revolt; five were executed and over a hundred exiled to Siberia. This was Russia's first organized noble revolutionary movement, profoundly influencing later revolutionary thought.

Under the leadership of independence hero Sucre, Upper Peru declared independence from Spain and named the new nation 'Bolivia' in honor of liberator Simón Bolívar. Bolívar personally drafted Bolivia's constitution, which established a strong presidential system. Independent Bolivia had a Pacific coastline but lost it in the 1879 War of the Pacific, becoming landlocked.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: French inventor Joseph Nicéphore Niépce, using a bitumen-coated pewter plate in a camera obscura with an exposure of about 8 hours, captured the world's first permanent photograph, 'View from the Window at Le Gras.' Though the image was blurry, it marked the birth of photography. Niépce later collaborated with Daguerre but died before perfecting the process.

Ottoman Sultan Mahmud II, seeking to establish a Western-style modern army, ordered the dissolution of the long-corrupt and repeatedly rebellious Janissary corps. The Janissaries mutinied in Istanbul, but Mahmud II had prepared his new artillery, which bombarded their barracks, killing about 6,000 and disbanding the corps. This 'Auspicious Incident' cleared the way for the Tanzimat reforms of the Ottoman Empire.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: A combined British, French, and Russian fleet destroyed the Ottoman-Egyptian fleet in Navarino Bay, Greece. This was the last major naval battle fought entirely with sailing ships; the allied fleet lost not a single vessel while the Ottoman-Egyptian fleet was nearly annihilated. The battle forced the Ottoman Empire to accept Greek autonomy, accelerating Greek independence and increasing Russian influence in the Balkans.

German physicist Georg Simon Ohm, in his work 'The Galvanic Circuit Investigated Mathematically,' systematically described the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance: V=IR. Initially rejected by the academic community, Ohm's law was later widely accepted and became a fundamental principle of circuit analysis. The unit of electrical resistance, the ohm, is named after him.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: Russia declared war on the Ottoman Empire, citing assistance to Greek independence and Ottoman treaty violations. Russian forces fought in the Danubian Principalities and the Caucasus, crossing the Balkan Mountains for the first time and threatening Constantinople. The Treaty of Adrianople (1829) awarded Russia territory on the Black Sea's eastern coast and control of the Caucasus, while the Ottoman Empire recognized Greek and Serbian autonomy.

Andrew Jackson decisively defeated incumbent President John Quincy Adams, marking the shift from the 'Era of Gentlemen' to 'mass politics' in American history. Jackson was the first president from the West and not among the Founding Fathers. He expanded presidential power, implemented the spoils system and Indian removal policy, and opposed the Second Bank. His election inaugurated the Jacksonian era of democracy.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: After Russia's defeat of the Ottoman Empire, the London Protocol recognized Greece as an independent kingdom. Bavarian Prince Otto was chosen as Greece's first king (Otto I), making Greece the first Christian nation to gain independence from the Ottoman Empire. Independent Greece was limited to the Peloponnese and the Cyclades, excluding Crete and Macedonia.

British inventor Sir Goldsworthy Gurney built a steam-powered road coach that operated between London and Bath at about 24 km/h. Though ultimately thwarted by turnpike laws and opposition from the horse-coach industry, this was the first successful use of steam power for road passenger transport, foreshadowing the automobile age.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: King Charles X of France issued the July Ordinances, dissolving parliament, restricting press freedom, and changing electoral law in an attempt to restore absolute monarchy. Parisians rose up, and after three 'Glorious Days' of fighting, overthrew the Bourbon Restoration. Charles X abdicated and fled to England. The financial bourgeoisie backed Louis-Philippe, Duke of Orléans, to establish the 'July Monarchy' under a constitutional framework.

Inspired by the French July Revolution, a revolt against Dutch King William I erupted in Brussels and rapidly spread nationwide. Dutch forces failed to suppress it. European powers convened the London Conference, agreeing to Belgian independence as a constitutional monarchy with permanent neutrality. Leopold I was selected as Belgium's first king.

French Romantic painter Eugène Delacroix created the iconic painting 'Liberty Leading the People,' inspired by the July Revolution of 1830. The allegorical figure of Liberty, bearing the tricolor flag, leads workers, intellectuals, and citizens over barricades. The painting became a symbol of French republican spirit and was later featured on the 100-franc banknote.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: British scientist Michael Faraday, through a series of experiments, discovered electromagnetic induction: a changing magnetic field can induce an electric current in a closed conductor. He built the world's first generator (Faraday's disk), providing a revolutionary method for converting mechanical energy into electricity, laying the theoretical foundation for the modern electrical power industry.

Italian nationalist Giuseppe Mazzini founded 'Young Italy' in Marseille, aiming to unify the numerous Italian states into a democratic republic through insurrection and revolution. The organization's motto was 'Unity, Independence, Liberty.' It nurtured leaders like Garibaldi and became the core of the Italian Risorgimento movement.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: The British Parliament passed the Reform Act of 1832, abolishing 56 'rotten boroughs' and redistributing seats to emerging industrial cities like Manchester and Birmingham. The electorate increased from about 500,000 to 810,000, with the middle class gaining voting rights for the first time. Although the working class remained excluded, the Act broke the landed aristocracy's monopoly on Parliament, marking a crucial step toward democratization.

Native American chief Black Hawk led Sauk and Fox tribes back to their ancestral lands in Illinois from west of the Mississippi, clashing with U.S. militia and regular forces. U.S. forces decimated the Native Americans at the Battle of Bad Axe, and Black Hawk was captured. The war forced local Native Americans entirely out of Illinois, accelerating the American westward expansion.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: The British Parliament passed the Slavery Abolition Act, freeing all slaves throughout the British Empire effective August 1, 1834. The act included a four-year 'apprenticeship' system (forced labor for former owners). The government spent £20 million (5% of British GDP) compensating slave owners. This decisive victory for the British abolitionist movement spurred global abolition efforts.

Benjamin Day founded the first successful penny press newspaper in the United States, the New York Sun. Priced at just one cent and sold on street corners, it featured sensational news, crime reports, and advertisements. Its circulation quickly surpassed traditional six-cent papers, ushering in the era of mass-circulation journalism and reshaping the news industry.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: Led by Prussia, 18 German states formed the Zollverein (German Customs Union), abolishing internal tariffs and unifying external tariff rates. This marked the beginning of German economic integration, greatly promoting industrial development and trade among the states and laying the economic foundation for eventual German political unification.

v.t: British Poor Law Amendment Act of 1834
v.s: The British Parliament passed the Poor Law Amendment Act, ending outdoor relief for able-bodied paupers. The poor were required to enter harsh workhouses for labor, aiming to reduce poor rates and compel self-reliance. The Act faced strong worker protests and was harshly criticized by writers like Charles Dickens in 'Oliver Twist.'

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.t: Andersen publishes first fairy tale collection
v.s: Danish author Hans Christian Andersen published his first collection of 'Fairy Tales Told for Children,' including four stories such as 'The Tinderbox' and 'Little Claus and Big Claus.' Unlike traditional fairy tales, Andersen creatively blended literary artistry with folk narratives. His works have been translated into over 150 languages.

v.t: Great Trek of the Boers begins
v.s: Discontented with British abolition of slavery and compensation policies, about 15,000 Boer (Dutch-descendant) settlers in the Cape Colony packed their belongings and livestock and migrated northward into the South African interior in what became known as the Great Trek. The Boers established independent republics including the Natalia Republic, Transvaal Republic, and Orange Free State.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: Texas settlers (mostly American-born) captured Mexican President Santa Anna at the Battle of San Jacinto, forcing him to sign a treaty recognizing Texan independence. The 'Lone Star Republic' was established with its capital in Austin. However, the Mexican Congress refused to recognize it, leaving the two nations on the brink of war for nine years. Texas was annexed by the United States in 1845.

The London Working Men's Association published the 'People's Charter,' proposing six political demands: universal male suffrage, secret ballot, abolition of property requirements for MPs, payment for MPs, equal electoral districts, and annual parliamentary elections. This was the world's first independent national political movement of the working class. Though all three petitions were rejected by Parliament, it advanced Britain's democratization.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: 18-year-old Alexandrina Victoria succeeded her uncle William IV as Queen of the United Kingdom. Her 64-year reign (until 1901) was among the longest in British history. Victoria strictly adhered to constitutional monarchy, becoming a symbol of national unity and morality. Her era witnessed the peak of the Industrial Revolution and the expansion of the British Empire to encompass a quarter of the world's territory, known as the 'Victorian Age.'

Osaka city official Oshio Heihachiro, outraged by corrupt shogunate rule and high rice prices, sold his possessions to purchase firearms and led poor townspeople and peasants in an uprising, burning wealthy merchants' homes and attacking government offices. The revolt was suppressed within a day, and Oshio committed suicide. This rare urban intellectual-led revolt in the Edo period signaled the crisis of shogunate rule.

American inventor Samuel Morse demonstrated the first practical telegraph in New York and developed Morse code with assistant Alfred Vail. On May 24, 1844, Morse sent the first official telegram from Washington to Baltimore: 'WHAT HATH GOD WROUGHT.' The telegraph system revolutionized communication, allowing messages to travel thousands of miles in minutes.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: Under the Indian Removal Act, the U.S. federal government forcibly removed about 16,000 Cherokee people from their homelands in Georgia and other southeastern states to Indian Territory (modern Oklahoma). Approximately 4,000 died en route from disease, cold, and starvation. The Cherokees' tragic journey became known as the 'Trail of Tears,' one of the most inhumane chapters of the westward expansion against Native Americans.

v.t: Oregon Trail opens for westward migration
v.s: American explorer Elijah White led the first wagon train along the Oregon Trail westward, opening the route for large-scale use. The Oregon Trail stretched about 3,200 km from Independence, Missouri to the Willamette Valley, becoming the primary route for hundreds of thousands of American westward migrants in the mid-19th century.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: Imperial Commissioner Lin Zexu publicly destroyed confiscated opium at Humen Beach, Guangdong. Using a method of soaking the opium in brine and lime, he destroyed over 20,000 chests (approximately 2.37 million jin or 1.18 million kg) over 23 days. The Humen opium destruction was the first righteous struggle against foreign aggression in modern Chinese history, but it directly triggered the First Opium War.

The French Academy of Sciences and Academy of Fine Arts jointly announced the acquisition of Louis Daguerre's daguerreotype process and released the technical details to the world free of charge. The daguerreotype used silver-iodide-sensitized plates, developed and fixed to produce permanent images, reducing exposure time from 8 hours to about 30 minutes. It was the first practical photographic process, marking the birth of modern photography.

In 1839, British Chartists submitted the first national petition to Parliament, with about 1.28 million signatures. The Chartist movement demanded the six points of the People's Charter: universal male suffrage, secret ballot, abolition of property qualifications for MPs, payment for MPs, equal constituencies, and annual elections. Parliament rejected the petition, sparking a series of protests. The Chartist movement was one of the world's earliest national working-class political movements.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: Britain, using the Humen opium destruction as a pretext, dispatched an expeditionary force to blockade the Pearl River estuary, capture Dinghai, and threaten the Dagu forts. The Qing military suffered repeated defeats. In 1842, the Treaty of Nanking was signed, ceding Hong Kong, opening five treaty ports, and paying 21 million silver dollars in indemnity. The Opium War marks the beginning of modern Chinese history and China's descent into a semi-colonial, semi-feudal society.

British Crown representatives and Māori chiefs signed the Treaty of Waitangi in the Bay of Islands, whereby Māori ceded sovereignty to Britain in exchange for British protection and rights as British subjects. However, significant discrepancies existed between the English and Māori versions (especially regarding sovereignty), leading to decades of land wars and disputes. The treaty is regarded as New Zealand's founding document.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: After British forces captured forts north of Guangzhou and looted the Sanyuanli area, villagers led by farmer Wei Shaoguang mobilized over 103 surrounding villages to ambush British troops at Niulangang during a thunderstorm, killing Major Bichard and dozens of others. The Sanyuanli resistance was the first large-scale spontaneous Chinese civilian struggle against foreign aggression, demonstrating the Chinese people's indomitable spirit.

British colonists forced Fijian chiefs to cede the islands, though the first annexation was not immediately recognized by the British government. In 1874, Britain formally declared Fiji a colony. Fiji became an important South Pacific center for sugar plantations and labor importation.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: Qing Imperial Commissioners Qiying and Yilibu signed the Treaty of Nanking aboard the British warship HMS Cornwallis at Xiaguan, Nanjing, with British Plenipotentiary Sir Henry Pottinger. This was the first unequal treaty in modern Chinese history, marking the end of the First Opium War and China's descent into semi-colonial status.

American painter and inventor Samuel Morse successfully transmitted the first public telegram, 'WHAT HATH GOD WROUGHT,' using his designed telegraph at New York University. He subsequently developed Morse code and opened the commercial Washington-to-Baltimore line in 1844, fundamentally changing long-distance communication.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.t: Supplementary Treaty of the Bogue signed
v.s: As supplements to the Treaty of Nanking, Britain and the Qing court signed the General Regulations for Trade and the Supplementary Treaty of the Bogue. Britain gained most-favored-nation status, consular jurisdiction, and the right to establish concessions in treaty ports. These provisions provided legal grounds for further Western expansion of privileges in China.

Denmark abolished the slave trade in its West Indies colonies (now U.S. Virgin Islands), prohibiting the importation of slaves. Existing slaves on the islands were not immediately freed. Denmark was one of the earlier European nations to abolish the slave trade, reflecting the influence of humanitarian thought.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: U.S. Commissioner Caleb Cushing and the Qing court signed the Treaty of Wanghia at Wangxia village near Macau. The United States gained most-favored-nation status equal to Britain's, plus additional privileges such as 'agreed tariffs' and port patrol. This was the first unequal treaty between China and the United States.

Samuel Morse and Alfred Vail completed the final design of Morse code. The code used dots, dashes, and spaces to represent letters and numbers, greatly simplifying telegraph transmission. The first official telegram, 'WHAT HATH GOD WROUGHT,' was sent in 1844, marking the dawn of the telecommunications era.

In 1844, 28 flannel weavers in Rochdale, Lancashire, England, founded the Rochdale Society of Equitable Pioneers, the world's first successful consumer cooperative. It sold food at fair prices and distributed annual dividends based on purchases. Its operational principles—voluntary membership, democratic management, dividend based on purchases—became the foundation of the international cooperative movement.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: The U.S. Congress passed a joint resolution formally annexing the Republic of Texas as the 28th state. Mexico broke off diplomatic relations, viewing the annexation of its breakaway province as a territorial violation, paving the way for the Mexican-American War in 1846.

The Irish potato crop failed for several consecutive years due to late blight, leading to approximately one million deaths from starvation and disease, and another million emigrating (mainly to the United States). This was one of the most severe famines in 19th-century Europe, reducing Ireland's population by a quarter and intensifying anti-British nationalist sentiment.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: The United States declared war on Mexico over a border dispute. U.S. forces under Zachary Taylor and Winfield Scott invaded Mexico, capturing Mexico City in 1847. The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (1848) forced Mexico to cede California, New Mexico, Arizona, and other vast territories (about 2.3 million sq km) to the United States—the most important turning point in U.S.-Mexico relations.

British Prime Minister Sir Robert Peel successfully pushed Parliament to repeal the Corn Laws, which had imposed high tariffs on imported grain since 1815 to protect the landed aristocracy. Repeal lowered food prices, reduced the burden on the working class, and marked Britain's fundamental shift from protectionism to free trade.

German astronomer Johann Gottfried Galle, following the mathematical predictions of French mathematician Urbain Le Verrier, discovered Neptune, the eighth planet in the solar system, at the Berlin Observatory. This was the first celestial body discovered through mathematical calculation rather than direct observation, confirming the universality of Newton's law of gravitation.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: The American Colonization Society, established to resettle freed American black slaves, declared the independence of the Liberia colony on West Africa's coast. Its constitution and government were modeled after the United States, with the capital Monrovia named after U.S. President James Monroe. Liberia was Africa's first republic but long suffered from tensions between Americo-Liberian settlers and indigenous peoples.

Charlotte Brontë's 'Jane Eyre,' Emily Brontë's 'Wuthering Heights,' and Anne Brontë's 'Agnes Grey' were published in quick succession. The sisters initially used male pseudonyms (Currer, Ellis, and Acton Bell) to avoid gender bias against women writers. These works became English literary classics, profoundly exploring female independence, love, and social class.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, commissioned by the Communist League, published 'The Communist Manifesto' in London. The manifesto systematically expounded historical materialism, class struggle theory, and the call 'Workers of the world, unite!', marking the birth of Marxism. It became the most influential document of the international workers' movement.

Gold was discovered at Sutter's Mill in California, and word spread, drawing about 300,000 people from across the U.S. and around the world, known as '49ers.' The Gold Rush spurred California's population explosion and economic development, accelerated railroad construction, but also led to genocide of Native Americans and environmental destruction. California was rapidly admitted to the Union in 1850.

Starting with an uprising in Sicily, revolution rapidly spread to France (overthrowing the July Monarchy), the German states, the Austrian Empire (Vienna Uprising, Hungarian Revolution), Italy, and other regions. Revolutionaries demanded national independence, constitutional freedoms, and social reform. Though ultimately suppressed, the revolutions severely weakened feudal forces, advancing abolition of serfdom and constitutional processes across Europe.

Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, commissioned by the Communist League, published 'The Communist Manifesto' in London. The manifesto systematically expounded historical materialism, class struggle theory, and the call 'Workers of the world, unite!', marking the birth of Marxism. It became the most influential document of the international workers' movement.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: After the Second Anglo-Sikh War, the British East India Company annexed Punjab (parts of modern northwestern India and Pakistan), captured Sikh Maharaja Duleep Singh, and exiled him. Punjab was incorporated into British India, and the Sikh Empire was destroyed. Britain thereby consolidated control over its northwestern frontier to contain Russian expansion.

During the 1848 revolutions, Pope Pius IX fled Rome, and Mazzini and Garibaldi established a republic implementing democratic reforms. Napoleon III sent troops to intervene and restore papal authority. The Roman Republic lasted only four months but became an important symbol of the Italian Risorgimento.

d.u: Emperor Xuanzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Minning)

v.s: The U.S. Congress passed a package of laws aimed at easing North-South tensions over slavery: California admitted as a free state; New Mexico and Utah territories organized on popular sovereignty; District of Columbia slave trade abolished; and a stricter Fugitive Slave Act enacted. The compromise temporarily postponed civil war, but the Fugitive Slave Act intensified Northern opposition.

v.t: Daoguang Emperor dies, Xianfeng Emperor ascends
v.s: The Daoguang Emperor died, and his fourth son Yizhu succeeded to the throne, adopting the reign title Xianfeng. He ascended amid the raging Taiping Rebellion and mounting pressure from Western powers. He attempted to revive the dynasty but failed to reverse its decline.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.s: Hong Xiuquan declared an uprising at Jintian Village, Guiping, Guangxi, establishing the 'Taiping Heavenly Kingdom.' The rebel army rapidly grew, sweeping across most of China and capturing Nanjing, which they renamed Tianjing as their capital, establishing a regime in opposition to the Qing. The Taiping issued the 'Land System of the Heavenly Dynasty,' advocating equal sharing of land. Internal division after the 1856 Tianjing Incident led to its eventual defeat by Qing forces in 1864.

The first World's Fair was held at the Crystal Palace in London's Hyde Park. The exhibition hall, designed by Joseph Paxton and built entirely of glass and iron, covered about 92,000 sq m and was dubbed the 'Crystal Palace.' It displayed approximately 100,000 exhibits, including advanced industrial machinery and crafts, showcasing Britain's industrial might as the 'workshop of the world.'

Henry Jarvis Raymond and George Jones founded the New-York Daily Times (later renamed The New York Times) in New York. Committed to serious, objective news reporting, it quickly became one of America's most influential newspapers.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.s: Louis-Napoléon Bonaparte (nephew of Napoleon I) proclaimed himself Emperor following a plebiscite, establishing the Second French Empire as Napoleon III. He pursued a policy of 'Empire means peace' yet also launched the Crimean War, intervened in Italian unification, and undertook the Mexican expedition. The Second Empire lasted until France's defeat in the Franco-Prussian War of 1870.

The Taiping Western Expedition captured Wuchang, the first provincial capital taken by the Taiping. They then advanced eastward along the Yangtze, capturing Nanjing in 1853 and renaming it Tianjing as their capital. The Taiping controlled the middle and lower Yangtze region, reaching their peak power.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.s: Russia, seeking control of the Balkans and the Black Sea straits, occupied the Ottoman Danubian Principalities (Moldavia and Wallachia). The Ottoman Empire declared war, and Britain and France joined the Ottoman side to prevent Russian expansion. Fighting took place mainly in Crimea, ending with the 1856 Treaty of Paris, which forced Russia to accept the neutralization of the Black Sea.

After capturing Nanjing, the Taiping renamed it Tianjing and established it as their capital. Hong Xiuquan moved into the Heavenly Palace and promulgated the 'Land System of the Heavenly Dynasty.' The establishment of the capital marked the Taiping's creation of a long-term regime opposing the Qing.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.t: Treaty of Kanagawa signed (Black Ships arrive)
v.s: U.S. Commodore Matthew Perry led his fleet into Edo Bay twice, using military threats to force the Tokugawa shogunate to sign the Treaty of Kanagawa (Japan-U.S. Treaty of Peace and Amity), opening the ports of Shimoda and Hakodate, and providing coal supplies and extraterritorial rights. Japan ended over 200 years of national seclusion and opened its doors.

v.t: Zeng Guofan forms the Xiang Army
v.s: To suppress the Taiping Rebellion, Zeng Guofan organized a local militia in Hunan known as the Xiang Army. He adopted Qi Jiguang's training methods, instilled Confucian loyalty, selected scholar-officers as commanders, and recruited sturdy peasants as soldiers. The Xiang Army became the main force that defeated the Taiping, setting a precedent for the growing power of regional governors.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.s: In the Crimean War, after an 11-month siege, the Anglo-French allies finally captured Russia's Black Sea Fleet base at Sevastopol. Russian losses were heavy, forcing them to seek peace. This war was the first to comprehensively utilize railways, telegraphs, rifled muskets, and field hospitals on a large scale.

The Taiping dispatched Lin Fengxiang and Li Kaifang with over 20,000 troops on a Northern Expedition that reached the vicinity of Tianjin. However, isolated deep in enemy territory, lacking supplies and reinforcements, they were completely destroyed by Qing forces. The Northern Expedition leaders were captured and executed. Though it failed, it diverted Qing forces and relieved pressure on Tianjing.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.s: Chinese naval forces arrested pirates aboard the Arrow, but British Consul Harry Parkes claimed the ship had been registered in Hong Kong and accused China of violating treaties. Britain used this as a pretext, jointly with France, to launch the Second Opium War. The war ended with Qing defeat and the signing of the Treaties of Tianjin and Beijing.

Internal power struggle erupted among Taiping leadership. Eastern King Yang Xiuqing demanded the title 'Ten Thousand Years,' prompting Heavenly King Hong Xiuquan to secretly order Northern King Wei Changhui to assassinate Yang and his followers. Wei then carried out a massacre. Wing King Shi Dakai returned to protest, and Wei attempted to kill him as well, forcing Shi to flee. Hong executed Wei. The Tianjing Incident severely damaged the Taiping, marking its decline from peak power.

British engineer Henry Bessemer announced his patented converter steelmaking process at the British Association for the Advancement of Science in London. By blowing air through molten pig iron to remove impurities, the process enabled large-scale, low-cost production of high-quality steel. The Bessemer process dramatically increased steel output and slashed prices, revolutionizing railways, bridges, and construction—a hallmark technology of the Second Industrial Revolution.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.s: Indian sepoys, angered by rifle cartridges greased with cow and pig fat (violating Hindu and Muslim taboos), launched a rebellion in Meerut that rapidly spread to Delhi, Kanpur, Lucknow, and elsewhere. The rebels proclaimed the last Mughal emperor, Bahadur Shah II, as their nominal leader. It took the British two years to fully suppress the rebellion. Subsequently, the British government abolished the East India Company's rule and established direct Crown control over British India.

During the Second Opium War, Anglo-French forces captured Guangzhou and took Governor-General Ye Mingchen prisoner. Guangzhou was occupied until 1861. The allied forces established a 'Allied Commission' to govern the city—the first time a Chinese city was under prolonged foreign military occupation.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.s: During the Second Opium War, Anglo-French forces captured the Dagu Forts and threatened Tianjin. The Qing court was forced to sign the Treaties of Tianjin with Britain, France, the U.S., and Russia, allowing permanent foreign legations in Beijing, opening 10 new treaty ports, granting foreigners the right to travel and preach in the interior, and paying indemnities. Ratification was interrupted by the Battle of Dagu and was not implemented until the 1860 Convention of Beijing.

v.t: British Crown takes control of India
v.s: After the Indian Rebellion, the British Parliament passed the Government of India Act, abolishing the East India Company's rule and placing India under direct Crown control, with a Secretary of State for India and a Governor-General (Viceroy). The Mughal Empire formally ended. Queen Victoria was proclaimed 'Empress of India' in 1876.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.t: Darwin publishes On the Origin of Species
v.s: Charles Darwin's landmark work, 'On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection,' was published. Darwin proposed the theory of natural selection—'survival of the fittest'—fundamentally changing humanity's understanding of nature and its own origins.

The Franco-Sardinian allied forces defeated the Austrian army at Solferino in a bloody battle. This victory prompted Napoleon III to negotiate peace with Austria (Villafranca Armistice), ceding Lombardy to Sardinia. The battle's horrific casualties also inspired Henri Dunant to found the International Red Cross movement.

v.t: Darwin's Origin of Species revolutionizes biology
v.s: On November 24, 1859, Charles Darwin published 'On the Origin of Species,' systematically explaining evolution through natural selection. Darwin argued that all species descended from common ancestors, gradually evolving under natural selection. The first edition of 1,250 copies sold out on the day of publication. Evolution theory is recognized as one of the most important scientific theories in human history, revolutionizing understanding of life and human origins.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.t: Convention of Beijing signed; Old Summer Palace destroyed
v.s: After further defeat in the Second Opium War, the Qing court signed the Convention of Beijing with Britain and France, recognizing the Treaties of Tianjin, opening Tianjin as a treaty port, ceding the Kowloon Peninsula to Britain, and paying massive indemnities. Anglo-French forces entered Beijing and set fire to the Old Summer Palace (Yuanmingyuan), looting countless treasures. This became one of the most painful humiliations in modern Chinese history.

Giuseppe Garibaldi led about a thousand 'Redshirts' from Genoa, landing in Sicily, liberating the island, crossing the Strait of Messina, and advancing on Naples. He facilitated the incorporation of the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies into the Kingdom of Sardinia, making a crucial contribution to Italian unification. Garibaldi patriotically handed over the South to King Victor Emmanuel II.

Abraham Lincoln was elected the 16th U.S. President as the Republican candidate. His opposition to slavery expansion led Southern states to view his election as a threat to the institution. South Carolina was the first to secede, followed by six other Southern states, forming the Confederate States of America in 1861, setting the stage for civil war.

d.u: Emperor Wenzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Yizhu)

v.s: Confederate forces bombarded Fort Sumter; Lincoln called for 75,000 volunteers to suppress the rebellion, marking the start of the American Civil War. The fundamental cause was the conflict between the Northern industrial capitalist system and the Southern slave-based plantation economy. The war ended in 1865 with Northern victory and the abolition of slavery.

Tsar Alexander II signed the Emancipation Reform, formally abolishing the centuries-old serfdom system. Approximately 23 million serfs gained personal freedom, the right to own property, marry, and engage in commerce, though peasants still had to pay redemption fees to landowners for land allotments. The reform was a crucial step in Russia's transition from feudalism to capitalism.

King Victor Emmanuel II of Sardinia was crowned King of Italy in Turin, formally establishing the Kingdom of Italy. The capital moved to Florence the following year and to Rome in 1871. Italian unification was largely complete, except for Venice and parts of the Papal States.

v.t: Xinyou Coup; Ci Xi begins regency
v.s: The Xianfeng Emperor died at the Rehe Summer Palace, leaving eight regents including Sushun to assist his young son. Empress Dowager Ci Xi, in alliance with Prince Gong (Yixin), staged the Xinyou Coup, executing Sushun and others, and established a regency with the two empress dowagers. Ci Xi effectively held supreme power in the Qing dynasty for 47 years until her death in 1908.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.s: King Wilhelm I of Prussia appointed Otto von Bismarck as Prime Minister. Bismarck pursued a 'blood and iron' policy, strengthening the Prussian military to achieve German unification through force. He launched the Second Schleswig War, the Austro-Prussian War, and the Franco-Prussian War, ultimately unifying Germany in 1871.

President Lincoln signed the Homestead Act, granting any adult citizen (including women and African Americans) 160 acres (about 65 hectares) of public land free of charge, provided they settled and cultivated it for five years. The Act promoted westward expansion and agricultural development but also accelerated the loss of Native American lands.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.s: President Lincoln formally signed and issued the Emancipation Proclamation, declaring all slaves in rebelling states (the Confederacy) to be free. The proclamation transformed the Civil War's purpose from preserving the Union to a moral crusade against slavery. It allowed African Americans to serve in the Union military, with about 180,000 black soldiers enlisting.

v.t: Battle of Gettysburg and the Gettysburg Address
v.s: The turning point of the American Civil War: Union forces defeated General Robert E. Lee's Confederate army at Gettysburg, with the South losing over a third of its forces. Four months after the battle, Lincoln delivered the famed Gettysburg Address at the national cemetery, articulating the democratic ideal of 'government of the people, by the people, for the people' in just 272 words—one of the greatest speeches in English.

Eleven football clubs and schools met at the Freemasons' Tavern on Great Queen Street, London, founding the world's first football governing body—the Football Association—and unifying the rules of football (banning carrying the ball with hands). This marked the birth of modern football.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.t: Fall of Tianjing ends the Taiping Rebellion
v.s: The Xiang Army breached Tianjing (Nanjing), ending the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom. The Heavenly King's palace was burned, and the Young Heavenly King Hong Tianguifu fled but was captured and executed. The Taiping Rebellion lasted 14 years, affected 18 provinces, and was the largest peasant uprising in Chinese history.

Worker representatives from Britain, France, Germany, Italy, and other countries gathered at St. Martin's Hall in London to establish the International Workingmen's Association (the First International). Marx attended and was elected to the General Council, drafting the Inaugural Address and Provisional Rules. The First International cultivated the first generation of international proletarian revolutionaries and was dissolved in 1876.

Twelve European states signed the 'Convention for the Amelioration of the Condition of the Wounded in Armies in the Field' (the first Geneva Convention) in Geneva, establishing the red cross emblem to protect wounded soldiers and medical personnel and requiring belligerents to care for the wounded. This marked the beginning of international humanitarian law.

Austrian monk Gregor Mendel presented his findings on heredity from pea hybridization experiments at the Natural History Society of Brno, formulating the laws of segregation and independent assortment. However, his research went unrecognized by the scientific community until 1900. Recognized as the 'father of genetics,' his work laid the foundation of modern genetics.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.s: Confederate General Robert E. Lee surrendered to Union General Grant, ending the Civil War. Five days later, Lincoln was shot in the head at Ford's Theatre in Washington by Southern sympathizer John Wilkes Booth, dying the next day. Lincoln became the first U.S. president to be assassinated; Vice President Andrew Johnson succeeded him.

The U.S. Congress passed the 13th Amendment to the Constitution, formally abolishing slavery. The amendment states: 'Neither slavery nor involuntary servitude... shall exist within the United States.' It was one of the most important achievements of the Reconstruction Era.

Austrian monk Gregor Mendel presented his paper 'Experiments on Plant Hybrids' at the Brno Natural History Society, articulating dominant and recessive traits, the law of segregation, and the law of independent assortment. His work went unnoticed until 1900, when it was rediscovered and became the foundation of modern genetics.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.s: Prussia, allied with Italy, defeated Austria at the Battle of Königgrätz (Sadowa). Prussia forced Austria to leave the German Confederation, dissolving the old Confederation and establishing the North German Confederation under Prussian leadership. Austria was excluded from German affairs, paving the way for Bismarck's eventual unification of Germany.

After multiple failures, a team led by American entrepreneur Cyrus West Field successfully laid a transatlantic submarine telegraph cable from Ireland to Newfoundland, enabling instant telegraphic communication between Europe and North America. The cable, described as the 'Victorian Internet,' greatly facilitated international exchange.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.s: Karl Marx completed Volume I of 'Das Kapital' (subtitled 'A Critique of Political Economy') in London, systematically expounding the theory of surplus value and revealing the internal contradictions and laws of capitalist production. The book profoundly influenced the international workers' movement and world history.

The British Parliament passed the British North America Act, merging the Province of Canada (Quebec and Ontario), New Brunswick, and Nova Scotia into the Dominion of Canada, with Ottawa as the capital. July 1 became Canada's national day. Other provinces subsequently joined, and Canada gradually gained more self-governing powers.

U.S. Secretary of State William Seward signed a treaty with the Russian minister, purchasing Alaska for $7.2 million (about 2 cents per acre). Americans mocked it as 'Seward's Folly,' but the 1896 Gold Rush and 20th-century oil discoveries proved its immense value.

The last Shogun, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, returned political authority to Emperor Meiji, ending Tokugawa shogunate rule. The following year, anti-shogunate forces staged a coup, declaring 'imperial restoration,' and the Meiji Restoration officially began. The restoration pursued the three policies of 'wealthy country, strong military, and civilization and enlightenment,' launching Japan's modernization.

Swedish chemist Alfred Nobel successfully invented dynamite in his laboratory near Stockholm by absorbing nitroglycerin into diatomaceous earth, making it stable and safe for handling and forming into sticks. Dynamite found extensive use in mining, tunneling, and railway construction. Nobel amassed a great fortune from dynamite and later established the Nobel Prizes in his 1895 will.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.t: Emperor Meiji issues the Charter Oath
v.s: Emperor Meiji issued the Five-Article Charter Oath, outlining the Meiji government's guiding principles: deliberative assemblies, unity of all classes, freedom for commoners to pursue their ambitions, abandonment of old customs, and seeking knowledge throughout the world. This oath became the Meiji government's policy platform, marking Japan's official entry into the Meiji era.

The U.S. House of Representatives impeached President Andrew Johnson for violating the Tenure of Office Act by removing Secretary of War Edwin Stanton. The Senate voted 35-19, one vote short of the two-thirds majority needed to convict, and Johnson was acquitted. This was the first presidential impeachment in U.S. history.

American inventor Christopher Sholes received a patent for the first practical typewriter in 1868. His design included the QWERTY keyboard layout, which separated commonly used letters to prevent jamming—a layout still used today. The Remington company began mass production in 1873. The typewriter revolutionized office work and document processing, creating new employment opportunities for millions of women.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.s: The Suez Canal, connecting the Mediterranean and Red Seas, officially opened. Constructed under the direction of Frenchman Ferdinand de Lesseps, the canal spans about 163 km. It shortened the sea route between Europe and Asia by about 7,000 km, greatly facilitating East-West trade. Britain subsequently became the canal's largest stakeholder.

v.t: Transcontinental Railroad completed in the U.S.
v.s: America's first transcontinental railroad was completed at Promontory Summit, Utah. The Central Pacific Railroad built from the west (Sacramento) and the Union Pacific Railroad from the east (Omaha), meeting at this point. The construction employed a large number of Chinese workers (approximately 12,000). The railroad unified the eastern and western United States, greatly stimulating economic development and westward expansion.

Russian chemist Dmitri Mendeleev published the first periodic table of elements, arranging elements by atomic weight and predicting the properties of undiscovered elements such as gallium, germanium, and scandium. The periodic table became the framework for chemical research and, with refinements, is still used today.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.t: Franco-Prussian War; Second French Empire collapses
v.s: France declared war on Prussia. Prussian forces under Moltke decisively defeated the French army, capturing Napoleon III at the Battle of Sedan. French civilians revolted, overthrowing the Second Empire and establishing the Third Republic. Prussian forces then besieged Paris, and the war continued into 1871.

The First Vatican Council passed 'Pastor Aeternus,' proclaiming papal infallibility when defining faith and moral doctrine ex cathedra. This was the Catholic Church's response to modernity and rising nation-states, but it also intensified tensions with secular governments.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.s: After the Franco-Prussian War, Prussian King Wilhelm I was crowned German Emperor in the Hall of Mirrors at the Palace of Versailles, formally establishing the German Empire (Second Reich). Bismarck became Chancellor. German unification altered the European balance of power, making Germany the most powerful nation on the continent.

On March 18, Parisian workers and the National Guard rose up, establishing the first proletarian government in human history—the Paris Commune. The Commune existed for 72 days, implementing radical reforms including separation of church and state and worker management of factories. It was ultimately crushed by French government forces, with about 20,000 killed or executed.

v.t: Japan abolishes domains and establishes prefectures
v.s: The Meiji government abolished over 260 feudal domains nationwide, replacing them with a centrally controlled prefecture system. Former domain lords (daimyo) were removed from administrative positions and relocated to Tokyo on fixed stipends. This move definitively ended the feudal system and achieved centralized governance.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.s: Japan's first railway (Shimbashi to Yokohama) opened, built under English engineers' guidance. The Meiji government vigorously promoted railway development, using this line as a model for learning Western technology. Railways played a crucial role in Japan's modernization, logistics, and military mobilization.

The Royal Society dispatched HMS Challenger on a four-year global oceanographic expedition, systematically measuring seawater temperature, salinity, currents, and collecting deep-sea organisms and sediment samples. This foundational voyage of modern oceanography discovered approximately 4,700 new species.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.s: A financial panic triggered by the Vienna stock market crash in Austria rapidly spread to Europe and the United States. The U.S. investment bank Jay Cooke & Company failed, hundreds of railroads and banks collapsed, and the U.S. entered a five-year depression with unemployment reaching 14%. It was one of the most severe economic crises of the 19th century.

The Meiji government enacted the Land Tax Reform Ordinance, abolishing feudal tribute and establishing a monetary tax system based on land value. The reform established modern private land ownership, increased government revenue, and provided capital for capitalist development, though it increased the burden on farmers.

d.u: Emperor Muzong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaichun)

v.s: Using the murder of Ryukyuan sailors as a pretext, Japan dispatched troops to invade southern Taiwan and engage indigenous peoples. After Qing government negotiations, Japan withdrew its forces, but the Qing court recognized Japan's action as a 'righteous undertaking to protect its people' and paid an indemnity of 500,000 taels of silver. This was Japan's first military expansion into Chinese territory.

French composer Georges Bizet's opera 'Carmen' premiered at the Opéra-Comique in Paris. Initial reception was lukewarm, but the work achieved enormous success after Bizet's death, becoming one of the most frequently performed operas worldwide. Its aria 'L'amour est un oiseau rebelle' (Love is a rebellious bird) remains widely beloved.

v.t: Tongzhi Emperor dies; Guangxu Emperor ascends
v.s: The Tongzhi Emperor (Zaichun) died without an heir. Empress Dowager Ci Xi installed her four-year-old nephew Zaitian (son of Prince Chun) as emperor, with the reign title Guangxu, while the two empress dowagers continued the regency. Guangxu's reign saw the Sino-French War, the First Sino-Japanese War, the Hundred Days' Reform, and the Boxer Rebellion.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: The French National Assembly passed a series of laws formally establishing the Third Republic (proclaimed in 1870). The constitution created a President, Senate, and Chamber of Deputies under a republican system. The Third Republic lasted until 1940, making it the longest-lasting republican regime in French history.

v.t: Japan and Russia sign Treaty of Saint Petersburg
v.s: Japan and Russia signed a treaty in St. Petersburg whereby Japan relinquished its claim to southern Sakhalin in exchange for the entire Kuril Islands chain. This reflected Japan's strategy of 'abandoning the north to develop the south,' aiming to concentrate resources on developing Hokkaido.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: Alexander Graham Bell received a patent for the telephone from the U.S. Patent Office, just hours ahead of rival Elisha Gray's application. Days later, Bell spoke the first telephone message to his assistant Watson: 'Mr. Watson, come here, I want to see you.' The telephone completely transformed long-distance communication.

Combined Lakota Sioux and Cheyenne forces annihilated Lt. Col. George Custer's battalion of the 7th Cavalry Regiment, killing Custer and 268 soldiers. This was the greatest Native American victory over the U.S. Army, but it prompted the government to send reinforcements that forced Native American surrender, ending the Plains Wars.

The Meiji government issued the Sword Abolishment Edict, prohibiting the samurai class from carrying swords, stripping them of their millennia-old emblem of status. This destroyed the socioeconomic foundation of the feudal samurai class, marking a crucial step in Japan's transformation from a feudal warrior society to a modern nation.

v.t: Otto invents four-stroke internal combustion engine
v.s: German engineer Nikolaus Otto successfully built the first four-stroke internal combustion engine (Otto cycle engine) in 1876. The engine used gas fuel and converted thermal energy to mechanical power through four strokes: intake, compression, power, and exhaust. The Otto engine was far more efficient than the steam engine, smaller and lighter, providing the power basis for automobiles and aircraft.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: Saigō Takamori led Kagoshima samurai in an anti-government rebellion opposing Meiji reforms that stripped samurai privileges. Government forces defeated Saigō at Mount Shiroyama after eight months, and Saigō was killed. This was Japan's last civil war; thereafter the samurai class disappeared completely, and Meiji government authority was firmly established.

v.t: Reconstruction ends in the United States
v.s: President Rutherford B. Hayes withdrew federal troops from the South after his inauguration, formally ending the Reconstruction era (1865-1877). Southern Democrats regained power and began implementing racial segregation and Jim Crow laws that disenfranchised African Americans.

Thomas Edison invented the world's first phonograph, using a tinfoil-wrapped cylinder to record and play back sound. He recorded 'Mary Had a Little Lamb,' the first recording in human history. The phonograph launched sound recording and reproduction technology.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: European powers convened in Berlin to revise the Treaty of San Stefano following the Russo-Turkish War, reducing Bulgaria's territory, granting Austria control over Bosnia and Herzegovina, and giving Cyprus to Britain. The Congress exacerbated Balkan ethnic tensions, laying the groundwork for the 1914 assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo.

Zuo Zongtang led Qing forces to eliminate the regime established by Yakub Beg, recovering all of Xinjiang (except the Ili region occupied by Russia). The Qing court subsequently established Xinjiang Province in 1884, strengthening governance in the northwestern frontier.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.t: Edison invents practical incandescent light bulb
v.s: After thousands of experiments, Thomas Edison successfully lit the first practical carbon-filament incandescent bulb, which burned for about 13.5 hours. Switching to bamboo filament in 1880 extended bulb life to 1,200 hours. Edison also created a complete electric lighting system. The electric light banished darkness from human life.

v.t: Dual Alliance signed between Germany and Austria
v.s: Germany and Austria-Hungary signed the Dual Alliance Treaty in Vienna, pledging mutual defense against Russian attack. This was the core of Bismarck's alliance system, later joined by Italy to form the Triple Alliance.
