The Meiji government abolished the Ryukyu Domain and established Okinawa Prefecture, formally annexing the Ryukyu Kingdom. China's Qing government protested without effect. The Ryukyu Kingdom, once a Chinese tributary state, lost all independence after Japanese annexation.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: Zeng Jize (son of Zeng Guofan) renegotiated the Treaty of Livadia with Russia, signing the Treaty of Saint Petersburg (Ili Treaty). Russia returned the Ili region to China but retained territory west of the Khorgos River, with an indemnity of 9 million rubles. China recovered some territory, marking one of the few successful diplomatic negotiations in the late Qing period.

v.t: Greenwich Mean Time established as standard
v.s: The International Meridian Conference established the Greenwich Observatory as the prime meridian, dividing the globe into 24 time zones. This standard time system was subsequently adopted globally, facilitating international travel and communication.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.t: Tangxu Railway completed (China's first self-built railway)
v.s: China's first self-built standard-gauge railway, the Tangxu Railway (Tangshan to Xugezhuang), was completed, spanning about 11 km. Initially pulled by mules, it later introduced steam locomotives. Built by the Kaiping Mining Bureau under the Self-Strengthening Movement to transport coal, it marked the beginning of China's railway industry.

Tsar Alexander II was assassinated by a bomb thrown by members of the revolutionary 'People's Will' organization in St. Petersburg. Though he had implemented serfdom reform, he failed to satisfy radical demands. His son Alexander III succeeded him and turned to reactionary policies, strengthening autocratic rule.

The U.S. Congress passed the Chinese Exclusion Act, banning Chinese labor immigration for 10 years and prohibiting Chinese from naturalization. This was the first and only U.S. immigration law specifically targeting a single ethnic group, reflecting West Coast racism and economic competition.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: Germany, Austria-Hungary, and Italy signed the Triple Alliance Treaty in Vienna, pledging mutual assistance if any party was attacked. The Triple Alliance and the later Triple Entente (Britain, France, Russia) formed the two opposing blocs that led to World War I.

German bacteriologist Robert Koch announced the discovery of the tuberculosis bacterium. He also discovered cholera vibrio and anthrax bacillus, and formulated Koch's postulates (standards for identifying pathogens). Koch received the 1905 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, laying the foundation for bacteriology.

Britain, using the pretext of suppressing the Egyptian Urabi Revolt against Western influence, occupied Egypt, effectively reducing it to a British protectorate (nominally still part of the Ottoman Empire). Control of the Suez Canal and Red Sea route fell into British hands. Egypt did not achieve nominal independence until 1922.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: France, seeking to control Vietnam, attacked Qing troops stationed there, sparking the Sino-French War. Qing forces achieved victories in Taiwan and at Zhennan Pass, but the court sought peace, ultimately signing the Treaty of Tianjin, abandoning suzerainty over Vietnam. France gained Vietnam as a colony.

v.t: Germany establishes modern social security system
v.s: The Bismarck government passed the Health Insurance Bill, followed by the Accident Insurance Bill and the Old Age and Disability Insurance Bill, creating the world's first modern social security system. Aimed at appeasing workers and countering the socialist movement, it objectively pioneered the welfare state system.

The Brooklyn Bridge opened in New York, featuring a main span of 486 meters, making it the world's first steel-wire suspension bridge. Designed and built by the Roebling family, it became a New York landmark.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: From November 1884 to February 1885, under Bismarck's chairmanship, 15 European states convened in Berlin to establish the 'effective occupation' principle for partitioning Africa. By 1914, all of Africa except Ethiopia and Liberia had been divided among European powers.

v.t: Greenwich Mean Time becomes international standard
v.s: The International Meridian Conference in Washington D.C. selected the Greenwich Observatory meridian as the prime meridian, with global time zones based on GMT. This was a milestone in international time standardization.

During the Sino-French War, the French Far East Fleet launched a surprise attack on the Fujian Fleet at Mawei Harbor, Fuzhou. The Qing forces suffered a disastrous defeat, with nearly their entire fleet lost. The French subsequently blockaded the Taiwan Strait but failed to occupy Taiwan.

British engineer Charles Parsons invented the first practical steam turbine in 1884. The steam turbine converted steam energy into rotary motion, more efficient and compact than reciprocating steam engines. Parsons built the experimental vessel 'Turbinia' (1897), which astonished the world at a naval review by reaching 34 knots. Steam turbines soon became the standard power plant for power stations and large ships.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: German engineer Karl Benz built the world's first gasoline-powered three-wheeled automobile, the 'Benz Patent-Motorwagen,' patented on January 29, 1886. It featured a 0.75 hp single-cylinder four-stroke engine with a top speed of about 16 km/h. The automobile fundamentally changed human transportation.

Retired British civil servant Allan Octavian Hume convened 72 delegates in Bombay to form the Indian National Congress. Initially advocating reforms within the British framework, it later became the principal party in the struggle for Indian independence. 1885 also marked the rise of the Indian nationalist movement.

Louis Pasteur administered the first human rabies vaccine to 9-year-old Joseph Meister, who had been severely bitten by a rabid dog. Meister fully recovered after 13 injections. This breakthrough marked the birth of microbiology and immunology.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: The Statue of Liberty, a gift from France to the United States, was officially unveiled in New York Harbor. Designed by sculptor Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi with an internal iron framework by Gustave Eiffel, the statue stands 46 m tall (93 m including pedestal), holding a torch and a tablet inscribed with the U.S. independence date. It became a symbol of freedom and democracy and one of America's most famous landmarks.

After the Third Anglo-Burmese War, Britain formally annexed Upper Burma, incorporating all of Burma into British India. The last Burmese dynasty, the Konbaung dynasty, fell. Burma remained under British colonial rule until its independence in 1948.

A labor rally in Chicago protesting police violence against strikers turned violent when a bomb was thrown, causing police casualties. Police opened fire, killing several workers and officers. Eight anarchists were convicted, four executed. This event became one of the origins of International Workers' Day (May Day).

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: American physicists Albert Michelson and Edward Morley conducted an experiment attempting to measure the 'ether' relative to Earth's motion. The results disproved the existence of the ether, laying the groundwork for Einstein's theory of relativity. Michelson received the 1907 Nobel Prize in Physics.

v.t: Sino-Portuguese Treaty of Amity and Commerce
v.s: The Qing court signed a treaty with Portugal, with Portugal promising 'perpetual occupation and governance of Macau' while China retained sovereignty over Macau. Portugal subsequently occupied Macau until 1999.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: The Beiyang Fleet was formally established at Liugong Island, Weihaiwei, comprising 25 warships including the two largest ironclads in Asia, Dingyuan and Zhenyuan. With over 4,000 officers and men, it was created by Li Hongzhang and was once the most powerful navy in Asia, but was entirely annihilated in the First Sino-Japanese War of 1894-1895.

Princess Isabel, Regent of Brazil, signed the Golden Law, abolishing slavery and freeing approximately 700,000 slaves. Brazil was the last country in the Americas to abolish slavery. The abolition angered coffee plantation owners, and a military coup two years later overthrew the monarchy to establish the Republic of Brazil.

The world's first professional football league—the Football League—was founded with 12 clubs, including Preston North End and Aston Villa. The league's establishment marked the beginning of professional football.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: The Eiffel Tower, designed and built by Gustave Eiffel, was completed just before the opening of the 1889 Paris Exposition. Standing 312 m tall (later extended to 330 m), it was the world's tallest structure at the time. Initially criticized by artists, it later became Paris's most iconic landmark.

Emperor Meiji promulgated the Constitution of the Empire of Japan (Meiji Constitution), establishing a constitutional monarchy while granting the emperor extensive powers. The first Imperial Diet was simultaneously convened. This was a milestone in Japan's political modernization.

Under Engels' guidance, socialist parties from various countries gathered in Paris to establish the Second International (Socialist International). The Second International promoted the establishment of International Workers' Day and spread Marxism, but split after the outbreak of World War I in 1914.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: The U.S. 7th Cavalry surrounded a band of Lakota Sioux at Wounded Knee Creek, South Dakota. When soldiers tried to disarm the Native Americans, a conflict erupted, resulting in the massacre of at least 150-300 Native American men, women, and children. This was the last major massacre of the American Indian Wars, marking the end of violent westward expansion against indigenous peoples.
The U.S. Congress passed the Sherman Antitrust Act, America's first antitrust law targeting monopolies and restraints of trade. The act was later used to break up monopolies such as Standard Oil.

v.t: Zhang Zhidong founds Hanyang Iron Works
v.s: Viceroy of Huguang Zhang Zhidong founded the Hanyang Iron Works in Wuhan, China's first modern integrated steel enterprise and one of the earliest steel mills in Asia. Using Western technology and equipment, it supplied rails for projects like the Beijing-Hankou Railway, marking the development of heavy industry in the later Self-Strengthening Movement.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: Russia and France signed the Franco-Russian Accord, forming a military alliance to counter the Triple Alliance. Both parties agreed to support each other if attacked by Germany or Austria-Hungary. This was a precursor to the Triple Entente.

James Naismith, a physical education teacher at a Massachusetts YMCA, invented basketball by nailing peach baskets to the balcony railing and using a soccer ball. He drafted 13 rules. Basketball quickly became popular worldwide as one of the most beloved sports.

Tsar Alexander III presided over the groundbreaking of the Trans-Siberian Railway. Stretching 9,289 km from Moscow to Vladivostok, built over 25 years, it is the world's longest railway line. The railway strengthened Russia's control over the Far East.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: Russian composer Tchaikovsky's ballet 'The Nutcracker' premiered in St. Petersburg. Initial reception was poor, but it later became the most popular Christmas ballet, with its 'Waltz of the Flowers' and 'Dance of the Sugar Plum Fairy' widely beloved.

France and Russia signed a military convention stipulating that if Germany attacked either party, the other would provide full support. The agreement signaled Europe's division into two military blocs, gradually moving toward war.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.t: New Zealand first country to grant women's suffrage
v.s: The Governor of New Zealand signed the Electoral Act, making New Zealand the world's first country to grant women the right to vote in national elections. The movement's leader, Kate Sheppard, collected a petition with over 30,000 signatures through years of campaigning. New Zealand women's suffrage set an example for the global women's rights movement.

Mao Zedong, future revolutionary leader and principal founder of the Chinese Communist Party and the People's Republic of China, was born in a peasant family in Shaoshanchong, Xiangtan County, Hunan Province. His father, Mao Yichang, was a hardworking and thrifty farmer, and his mother, Wen Qimei, was kind and devout. Mao received traditional Confucian education in a Shaoshan private school in his youth, later studying in Xiangxiang and Changsha, gradually encountering new ideas.

Commemorating the 400th anniversary of Columbus's 'discovery' of America, Chicago hosted a world's fair in the Beaux-Arts style, attracting 27 million visitors. The exposition featured the first demonstration of alternating current lighting systems, the Ferris wheel, and other innovations.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: The Japanese Combined Fleet attacked a Chinese troop transport at Pungdo Island off Korea, sparking the First Sino-Japanese War. The war included the Battle of Pyongyang, the Battle of the Yalu River, the Port Arthur Massacre, and the Battle of Weihaiwei, ending with the Beiyang Fleet's total destruction. The Treaty of Shimonoseki (1895) ceded Taiwan and Penghu to Japan and imposed a 200 million-tael indemnity. The war shattered the Self-Strengthening Movement's illusion of 'restoration through strength.'

Sun Yat-sen founded the Revive China Society (Xingzhonghui) in Honolulu, Hawaii, proposing the platform of 'expelling the barbarians, restoring China, and establishing a united government.' It was China's first bourgeois revolutionary group, later forming the basis for the Tongmenghui.

French Jewish officer Alfred Dreyfus was wrongly convicted of treason and exiled to Devil's Island. The case exposed French anti-Semitism and military corruption. Writer Émile Zola published 'J'accuse...!' in defense. Dreyfus was eventually exonerated. The affair profoundly influenced French politics and intellectual positions.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: Qing Plenipotentiary Li Hongzhang and Japanese Prime Minister Ito Hirobumi signed the Treaty of Shimonoseki at Shimonoseki, Japan. China recognized Korean independence, ceded Taiwan and the Penghu Islands, paid 200 million taels in indemnity, and opened four treaty ports. The treaty's humiliating terms galvanized the reform movement.

German physicist Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen discovered X-rays (then called 'new rays') during experiments and took the world's first X-ray photograph of human bones (his wife's hand). X-rays revolutionized medical diagnosis. Röntgen received the first Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901.

News of the Treaty of Shimonoseki reached Beijing. Kang Youwei and Liang Qichao organized over 1,300 provincial candidates to submit a joint petition to the Guangxu Emperor, proposing rejection of peace, relocation of the capital, and political reform. This petition marked the beginning of the Hundred Days' Reform movement.

v.t: Lumière brothers hold first public film screening
v.s: The Lumière brothers screened short films including 'Workers Leaving the Lumière Factory' and 'The Arrival of a Train' at the Grand Café on Boulevard des Capucines in Paris, marking the birth of cinema. This invention launched modern film art and the entertainment industry.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.t: First modern Olympic Games held in Athens
v.s: From April 6 to April 15, the first modern Olympic Games were held in Athens, Greece, spearheaded by Baron Pierre de Coubertin. 311 athletes from 13 countries competed in 9 sports. American James Connolly won the first modern Olympic gold medal. The revival of the Olympics promoted international sports exchange.

v.t: Henry Ford builds his first car
v.s: Henry Ford built his first car, the Ford Quadricycle, in Detroit, powered by a gasoline engine with bicycle wheels. Ford subsequently founded the Ford Motor Company and pioneered the automobile assembly line production model.

Emperor Menelik II of Ethiopia led his army to defeat Italian invading forces at the Battle of Adwa, forcing Italy to recognize Ethiopian independence. This was the only battle in which an African nation decisively defeated a European colonial power and maintained its sovereignty.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: Using the Juye Incident as a pretext, Germany sent forces to occupy Jiaozhou Bay in Shandong. The following year, Germany signed the Jiaozhou Bay Concession Treaty with the Qing court, leasing the area for 99 years. Germany built a naval base and colonial city at Qingdao. This marked the beginning of the 'Scramble for Concessions' in China.

British physicist J.J. Thomson discovered through experiments that cathode rays were composed of negatively charged particles (electrons) and measured the electron's charge-to-mass ratio. This discovery showed that atoms were divisible, launching particle physics. Thomson received the 1906 Nobel Prize in Physics.

Theodor Herzl convened the First Zionist Congress in Basel, Switzerland, establishing the World Zionist Organization. The congress proposed establishing a Jewish national home in Palestine, laying the ideological and organizational foundation for the later founding of Israel.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: Emperor Guangxu, urged by Kang Youwei and Liang Qichao, issued the 'Imperial Decree on National Affairs' to begin reforms, including streamlining government, abolishing the eight-legged essay, establishing the Imperial University, encouraging industry and commerce, and training a new army. The reforms threatened conservative interests. On September 21, Empress Dowager Ci Xi staged a coup, imprisoned the Emperor, executed six reform leaders, ending the 103-day reform.

The United States declared war on Spain following the explosion of the USS Maine. The U.S. seized the Philippines, Guam, Puerto Rico, and Cuba. America emerged as a world power; Cuba gained nominal independence under U.S. protection, while the Philippines descended into an independence war.

The Qing government founded the Imperial University of Peking as one of the achievements of the Hundred Days' Reform. It was China's first modern national university, renamed Peking University in 1912, becoming the cradle of Chinese higher education.

Marie Curie and Pierre Curie isolated the radioactive element radium from uranium ore, a major breakthrough in radioactivity research. The Curies shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with Henri Becquerel. Marie Curie later became a two-time Nobel laureate.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: The Boxer movement, with the slogan 'Support the Qing, destroy the foreign,' rapidly developed in Shandong and Zhili provinces, burning churches, killing missionaries, and destroying railways and telegraph poles. In 1900, Boxers entered Beijing and besieged the Legation Quarter. Empress Dowager Ci Xi used the Boxers to declare war on the foreign powers, leading to the Eight-Nation Alliance invasion.

U.S. Secretary of State John Hay sent notes to the great powers calling for the preservation of China's territorial integrity and equal trading opportunities for all nations. The policy aimed to protect American commercial interests and prevent China from being completely partitioned, objectively curbing the powers' partition frenzy to some extent.

Britain and the South African Boers (of Dutch descent) went to war over gold mine rights and independence. The Boers waged guerrilla warfare, while the British used scorched-earth tactics and concentration camps, resulting in about 28,000 Boer civilian deaths. Britain ultimately prevailed, annexing the Transvaal and Orange Free State.

d.u: Emperor Dezong of Qing (Aisin Gioro Zaitian)

v.s: An eight-nation alliance of British, American, German, French, Russian, Japanese, Italian, and Austrian forces of about 20,000 advanced from Tianjin to Beijing, capturing the capital. Empress Dowager Ci Xi fled with the Guangxu Emperor to Xi'an. The allied forces looted and burned in Beijing, suppressed the Boxers, and forced the Qing court to sign the Boxer Protocol, with an indemnity of 450 million taels of silver.

v.t: Freud publishes The Interpretation of Dreams
v.s: Austrian psychologist Sigmund Freud's 'The Interpretation of Dreams' was published, proposing that dreams represent 'wish fulfillment' and establishing psychoanalytic theory. Initially selling very poorly, it later became one of the 20th century's most influential psychological works.

German physicist Max Planck proposed the quantum hypothesis, suggesting that energy is emitted and absorbed in discrete quanta. This marked the birth of quantum mechanics, and Planck received the 1918 Nobel Prize in Physics.

Paris's first metro line (Line 1) opened, connecting Porte de Vincennes and Porte Maillot. Using electric traction and rubber tires, it became a model for modern metro systems, serving the Paris Exposition.

To resolve the 'ultraviolet catastrophe' of black-body radiation that classical physics could not explain, German physicist Max Planck presented his paper to the German Physical Society on December 14, proposing that energy is emitted and absorbed discontinuously in discrete 'energy quanta.' Planck's formula perfectly matched experiments, marking the birth of quantum mechanics. This theory overturned the Newtonian continuous worldview, becoming one of the two pillars of modern physics.

German inventor Ferdinand von Zeppelin's rigid airship LZ 1 made its first manned flight on July 2 over Lake Constance. The airship was 128 m long, with an aluminum frame and hydrogen gas cells, powered by two 16-hp engines, flying about 18 minutes covering 3.7 km. Though initial performance was limited, Zeppelin airships later developed into transatlantic commercial aviation tools and strategic bombers, ushering in the age of rigid airships.

On June 22, Daoist priest Wang Yuanlu, while clearing the corridor of Cave 16 at the Mogao Caves in Dunhuang, discovered a hidden wall behind which lay a small chamber filled with scrolls (now numbered Cave 17). The cave contained about 50,000 items including manuscripts, printed texts, paintings, and artifacts from the 4th to 11th centuries, in Chinese, Tibetan, Sanskrit, and other languages. Known as the 'Dunhuang Manuscripts,' this was one of the 20th century's most important archaeological discoveries, giving rise to the international field of 'Dunhuang Studies.' However, large quantities were subsequently taken abroad by Aurel Stein, Paul Pelliot, and others.

v.t: Freud publishes The Interpretation of Dreams
v.s: In 1900, Austrian psychiatrist Sigmund Freud published 'The Interpretation of Dreams' (Die Traumdeutung). Freud proposed that dreams are the 'royal road to the unconscious,' expounding on the structure of the unconscious psyche, the Oedipus complex, repression, and sublimation. The book sold only 351 copies in its first six years but gradually became one of the most influential psychological works of the 20th century, founding the psychoanalytic school.
